dialogue.utterance: Unit 3 responding for unconscious or fainting at 407 in city of [CITY].
Hello sir, I'm Karen, an EMT. Who called 911 and why?
My right arm is numb and has been since after 6, getting worse.
Can you follow me with your eyes and nod your head, George?
Your eyes are open and focused. Can you tell me your name?
I'm George... It's either 2012 or 2013, not sure.
Can you squeeze my finger with your left hand for me?
Can you tell me where we are right now?
I think [STATE]... This house, [CITY]. I had to re-learn to write left-handed after my stroke.
What is the year and when did your stroke happen?
I think 2012 or 2013... Not sure if the stroke was two years ago or two months ago, but I had a lot of left-side weakness after.
Your airway is clear and you are speaking well. Can you talk in full sentences?
Watch me as we count your breaths for accurate rate and depth, please.
Radial pulse is strong, 73 beats per minute while seated.
16 respirations per minute, non-labored and regular, nice job breathing in rhythm.
Blood pressure 120 over 66 while sitting in bed, within your normal range.
Oxygen saturation is 96 percent, which is good, well oxygenated for now.
I'm applying EKG leads to monitor your heart rhythm, it's a quick placement.
Glucose is normal at 92 mg/dL, no sign of hypoglycemia right now.
Any pain or numbness in other areas of your body, such as the face or legs, George?
Just numbness in the arm. No pain or other symptoms at all that I can think of.
You've had dementia and a CVA. Has the numbness been this way since then, or is it different now?
I have numbness, but it was always there after the stroke. I just get by with my left hand daily.
Can you describe the numbness you feel in words, like is it tingling, dull, or burning?
It doesn't burn or hurt, just numbness. I don't feel the hand at all sometimes.
On a scale of 0 to 10, how bad do you feel it is right now, George?
Zero; I don't feel pain at all, honestly. Just the numbness continues to bug me a bit more now.
No distress, no effort. You are breathing easily, which is reassuring and fine right now.
Skin tone is normal, warm, and dry. Any new cuts or signs of bleeding I should know about, George?
Shrugs "No, not lately."
Pupils are equal and reactive but I won't light it for testing. Let's test again: open your eyes and tell me what you see in the room. George: "a light... on the wall." Closed and reopened normally.
Why did you decide to call 911 tonight?
I think December... Maybe still December.
What was your main reason for calling 911 again, not just the numbness, but the fear of stroke?
I guess I didn't want to wait any longer. I saw a commercial and said, "That's me," and thought I better call before I forget again.
I'm going to listen to your lungs. Will you raise your shirt, please?
George keeps shirt on but rolls it up slightly.
Breathing sounds are clear and equal on both sides. No wheezes, crackles or other adventitious sounds.
IV has been placed in your arm for access. You'll feel a minor prick, but we've secured the site safely now.
Retaking pulse: 72 per minute, still strong and steady in hand at LAC site.
Breathing is 19 per minute now, still fine. Now with elevated minute volume from assistance but within acceptable range.
Blood pressure increased to 147 over 82. It's higher than baseline, but we'll continue to monitor closely.
And there's 18 respirations now after reclining and still regular without signs of fatigue.
Oxygen saturation remains at 95. You're well-oxygenated but let me check once more in a bit to confirm.
Sinus rhythm continuing on the monitor, as expected. No new changes showing currently on the heart monitor.
Activating protocol 3-5 for stroke/TIA, based on your baseline deficits in right arm function.
Saline lock secured in the IV in case of any treatment requirement en route. You're all set if we need to do a medication push.
We'll help you to sit and then walk over to the stretcher. Can I assist you to sit up comfortably?
Let's stand you up gently. Hold on my shoulder for balance. Your gait is steady, George.
You are now secured on the stretcher with five-point restraints. Moving from bed to stretcher with a pivot maneuver as per safety protocol.
You chose [COMPANY_NAME]. I'll confirm the ETA now as we begin moving to the ambulance.
IV and monitor are in place and safe for transport. All equipment set for movement without interruption.
How do you feel about the numbness now-still zero?
Yeah, 0, but it feels more like a buzz, maybe, down in the hand or fingers now as we wait.
72 beats per minute steady. No changes in rhythm or rate from the previous check.
18 per minute since the position change, still under control. No signs of distress or strain, just rhythm regulated.
Now at 117 over 66 and very stable, within expected range for a 77-year-old with baseline pressure.
Oxygen is back to 96. That's a good sign for overall oxygenation, especially with baseline pulmonary health.
Monitoring continues normal sinus rhythm. Still no visible arrhythmia or ischemic intervals showing.
You are now in a comfortable, well-secured position. Ready for transport to the hospital.
ECG and IV monitoring running as we transport. You will arrive with everyone ready for immediate assessment.
We tracked patient vitals and documented all interventions for ED. All notes and meds already prepped for handover.
Radioed ahead-ED aware and awaiting stable patient transfer. Call has been made, and they are aware of his baseline and condition.
Final pulse: 69-stable for transport completion. Transferring onto the gurney using a pull sheet method, as you requested.
George, try not to move your head-rest it where it's at and try to drop your left hand over the edge. That might make you more comfortable during the ride.
Everything is locked down: IV, patient, and monitoring equipment. Safe to move now.
Initial transport done with zero incident reported in vitals or patient behavior. Without alarms or changes in condition, we proceed as routine.
Blood pressure 117 over 66 after 5 minutes of transport, no spikes or challenges. Stable transportation rhythm.
96 saturation consistent with last check-O2 remains adequate for now.
EKG strips clear with no new patterns. We're safe for the remaining stretch.
Ensuring your arm is not pressing under you and the restraints are not moving or affecting IV site.
George, try not to move your head-rest it where it's at and try to drop your left hand over the edge. That might make you more comfortable during the ride.
Traffic is going well-ETA to [COMPANY_NAME] is 5 minutes. Advanced team is prepared at the door as noted on the incoming call.
ED team has been talking to us already. They have received clarification around intracranial concerns due to baseline alterations.
We'll be parking, and once on the loading area, the receiving team will meet us at the ambulance door for confirmation.
Final IV check and pulse reassurance begins as we prepare traffic stop.
Any issues or strange feelings with the IV now? George: "Feels still there, I don't notice it."
Still numb but no pain, right?
Yes, it's a background feeling but not an emergency-so I still say 0 as we roll.
Counting 69 beats at final check. All signs predictive of safe arrival upon exit.
Still 18 per minute and breathing pattern calm. No signs of distress or labored effort.
96, consistent again-great response with minimal O2 burden or re-breathing.
Driveway confirms clear approach. Unloading area secured with wheelchair and bed ready for direct-to-gurney transfer.
'Beshay, you got her, George is on the way and arriving shortly,' I say over radio.
We've documented 5 total vitals. No changes in state of mind. Baseline deficits unchanged since arrival.
Emergency medications, print of all treatments with times. All reviewed and cleared for handover.
Once you are on the bed, you'll be met by actual staff, and we will get your IV over to the hospital right away.
ED nurse confirms all meds flagged-Aricept and Prednisone noted in chart.
Transfer minimized: transfer from EMS stretcher to bed. Lying on bed, verbal recognized per normal.
CPR bag, SPO2, IV, and care notes stacked to side of bed ready for review.
They've confirmed sirens off and lights off on arrival at the door. No incident reported.
Our care ended here, but we've noticed no changes. EEG rhythm stays stable.
All wheels down and back-seems like we're ready for them now.
George, they'll take care of the rest now. Safe and sound you are.
Time of care room departure: 01:00.
Scene cleared with visitor standing by to sign off assistance at this location.
Considering the history of dementia, the barriers to care include limited reliability in patient self-report due to cognitive decline.
Baseline resp is 16 from initial assessment. George has no acute changes noted in breathing since baseline.
